translatedText: Asserting 'Fire' as the Archē of the World
Argued that the unchanging foundational substance making up the world is 'fire', and viewed the world as existing as living fire.
Proposing the Principle of Opposites
Presented the principle that opposing elements such as good and evil, life and death, struggle with each other to maintain balance and generate existence.
Presenting the Concept of Logos
Weakened the status of the senses by proposing the concept that the generation of all things occurs in accordance with Logos.
Who is Heraclitus?
Heraclitus was a pre-Socratic philosopher of ancient Greece.
When was Heraclitus born?
The date of his birth is highly uncertain, but records indicate he was in his 40s around 504 BC.
What are Heraclitus's main ideas?
Famous for saying 'No man ever steps in the same river twice', he argued that all things are in constant flux and that the foundational substance (archē) of the world is 'fire'.
By what nickname was Heraclitus known?
He was known in antiquity as 'the obscure philosopher' (Skoteinos) because he often wrote texts that were difficult to understand.
Where can Heraclitus's works be found?
His ideas appear in the works of Plato.
570 BC
Born on the island of Samos
532 BC
Fled his homeland, the island of Samos, due to the tyranny of Polycrates and settled in Crotone in southern Italy
Around 530 BC
Moved to Crotone in southern Italy and founded a religious school
495 BC
Death
Pythagorean Theorem
He is best known for the famous theorem named after him, the Pythagorean theorem.
First Use of the Term 'Philosopher'
He is said to be the first person to call himself a 'philosopher', meaning a lover of wisdom.
Asserting That Numbers Are the Source of All Things
He claimed that numbers are the fundamental principle of all things, attempting to prove that the universe is an ordered whole governed by a single law.
Establishing Music Theory
He argued that the purpose of music is to purify the soul, and explained musical consonance through the proportional lengths of strings.
Founding the Pythagorean School
He founded the Pythagorean school in Crotone, southern Italy, cultivating many young men to become philosophers and politicians.
When was Pythagoras born?
Pythagoras was born in 570 BC on the island of Samos.
What is Pythagoras best known for?
Pythagoras is best known for the famous mathematical theorem named after him, the Pythagorean theorem.
What did Pythagoras call himself?
Pythagoras is said to have been the first person to call himself a philosopher, a lover of wisdom.
What were the main religious tenets of the Pythagorean school?
The main doctrines of Pythagorean religion were the belief in the transmigration of souls and the prohibition against eating beans.
Where did Pythagoras establish his school?
Pythagoras moved to Crotone in southern Italy and established a religious school.
Birth of Ramesses II
Ramesses II ascended to the throne as Pharaoh
Fought the Battle of Kadesh against the Hittite king Muwatalli II
Signed a peace treaty and a truce between Egypt and the Hittites
Death of Ramesses II
Expansion of Egyptian Power
During his reign, Egypt prospered and expanded its power to Libya, Nubia, and Palestine.
Maintaining Peace with the Hittites
He entered into a truce with the Hittites, maintaining peace for 50 years.
Construction of Monumental Architecture
He built massive structures such as the temples of Abu Simbel, Karnak, and the Ramesseum throughout Egypt.
Erecting Victory Monuments
He launched an expedition beyond the First Cataract of the Nile into Nubia and erected many victory monuments.
When was Ramesses II born and when did he die?
Ramesses II was born in 1313 BC and died in 1213 BC.
Which Pharaoh of Egypt was Ramesses II?
Ramesses II was the third Pharaoh of the Nineteenth Dynasty of the New Kingdom of Egypt.
What was the period of Ramesses II's reign?
Ramesses II reigned from 1279 BC to 1213 BC.
What major battle did Ramesses II participate in?
Ramesses II fought the Battle of Kadesh against the Hittite king Muwatalli II in 1274 BC.
What important treaty was signed during the era of Ramesses II?
In 1268 BC, the 21st year of his reign, Ramesses II concluded a peace treaty with the Hittites.
What major structures did Ramesses II build?
Ramesses II constructed massive architectural works throughout Egypt, including the temples of Abu Simbel, Karnak, and the Ramesseum.
Is there a claim that the Pharaoh of the Exodus in the Bible is Ramesses II?
Although the Book of Exodus does not mention the Pharaoh's name, some biblical scholars assume that Ramesses II was the Pharaoh of the Exodus.
What is the meaning of the name Ramesses II?
The name Ramesses is the Greek pronunciation of 'Ra-mes-sis', which means 'born of Ra'.
Around 460 BC
Diogenes of Apollonia is a pre-Socratic philosopher from Athens.
Around 412 BC - Around 323 BC
Diogenes of Sinope is a philosopher of the Cynic school.
Around 230 BC - Around 150 BC
Diogenes of Babylon is a philosopher of the Stoic school.
3rd Century
Diogenes Laërtius is a biographer of ancient Greek philosophers.
Diogenes of Laertes is a character in the Greek epic poem, the Odyssey.
Is Diogenes a single historical figure?
No. According to the original source, there are multiple figures named Diogenes, including Diogenes of Apollonia, Diogenes of Sinope, Diogenes of Babylon, Diogenes Laërtius, and Diogenes of Laertes.
Who are the philosophers named Diogenes?
They include Diogenes of Apollonia (a pre-Socratic philosopher), Diogenes of Sinope (a Cynic philosopher), and Diogenes of Babylon (a Stoic philosopher).
September 7, 1533
Born as the daughter of Henry VIII and Anne Boleyn
September 10, 1533
Baptized
Her mother, Anne Boleyn, was executed
Restored to the line of succession through a change in the Act of Succession
After the death of Henry VIII, her half-brother Edward VI succeeded to the throne
May 1548
Sent away from home by her stepmother, Catherine Parr
September 5, 1548
Her stepmother, Catherine Parr, died
January 1549
Thomas Seymour was imprisoned in the Tower of London after his plot was discovered
March 20, 1549
Thomas Seymour was beheaded
July 6, 1553
Edward VI died
August 3, 1553
Rode triumphantly into London alongside Mary I
March 18, 1554
Imprisoned in the Tower of London after being suspected of complicity in Wyatt's rebellion
November 16, 1558
Mary I recognized Elizabeth as her heir
November 17, 1558
Following the death of Mary I, she ascended the throne as Queen of England and Ireland
January 15, 1559
Crowned at Westminster Abbey
The new Act of Supremacy was passed
Amy, the wife of Robert Dudley, died
October 1562
Invaded Le Havre in France
Robert Dudley was created Earl of Leicester
Mary, Queen of Scots, married Henry Stuart, Lord Darnley
Mary, Queen of Scots, gave birth to a son, James
Early February 1567
Lord Darnley was murdered
Mary, Queen of Scots, fled to England
A major Catholic rebellion, the Rising of the North, broke out in Northern England
Pope Pius V declared Elizabeth I an illegitimate heretic
Robert Dudley secretly married Lettice Knollys
Anti-Catholic penal laws were enacted
The rebellion of Gerald FitzGerald broke out in Munster
Humphrey Gilbert established a colony in North America
